questions.question: Define an Operating System and explain its three primary objectives/purposes in computer science.
keyPoints: OS as Resource Manager: manages CPU, memory, I/O devices efficiently
OS as Extended Machine: provides abstractions hiding hardware complexity
OS as User Interface: offers CLI/GUI for user-computer interaction
Facilitates program execution, system utilization and convenient usage

questions.question: Explain the concept of kernel in operating systems. Differentiate between monolithic kernel and microkernel architectures, highlighting the advantages of microkernel design.
keyPoints: Kernel is the core component running in privileged/ supervisor mode
Monolithic kernel: all OS services reside in kernel space, large size, high performance but less maintainable
Microkernel: minimal kernel with services in user space (IPC, basic scheduling, memory management)
Microkernel advantages: modularity, extensibility, better security/isolation, fault tolerance, easier debugging

questions.question: Describe the layered architecture of an operating system. Explain the functions of each major layer from hardware level to the user interface level. Also, compare the layered approach with the monolithic kernel architecture.
keyPoints: Hardware Layer: physical devices (CPU, memory, I/O controllers)
Device Driver Layer: interacts with hardware devices, handles I/O operations
Kernel Layer: process management, memory management, file system, inter-process communication
System Call Interface: provides API for user programs; Comparison: layered offers modularity and ease of development but overhead; monolithic offers better performance but complex and less secure

questions.question: Discuss device management as a core function of the operating system. Explain the techniques used for device allocation, device scheduling, and buffering. Additionally, elaborate on the security and protection mechanisms that must be implemented in modern operating systems.
keyPoints: Device Management: handles device allocation (dedicated, shared, virtual), scheduling (FCFS, SSTF, C-SCAN) and buffering to improve performance
I/O Handling: uses device drivers, interrupt handling, DMA for efficient I/O operations
Security Mechanisms: user authentication, authorization, access control lists (ACL), encryption, audit trails
Protection Mechanisms: memory protection (base/limit registers, paging), process isolation, dual-mode operation (user/kernel mode), protection rings
